file_name: Gareau et al. - 2020 - Deep learning-level melanoma detection by interpretable machine learning and imaging biomarker cues.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used both deep learning (Convolutional Neural Networks/CNN) and interpretable machine learning (Ensemble Classifier/Eclass) to discriminate melanomas from nevi in dermoscopy images. The study compared the diagnostic performance (AUROC) of these AI models against each other and against human pattern recognition methods for melanoma detection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of an interpretable machine learning algorithm (Eclass) and a deep learning classifier (CNN) for melanoma detection. It includes specific sections for Methods (describing IRB-approved human subjects research, data collection, and statistical analysis) and Results (reporting AUROC metrics, confidence intervals, and performance comparisons on validation sets), indicating it is an original study rather than a review.

title: Deep learning-level melanoma detection by interpretable machine learning and imaging biomarker cues
brief_summary: This study developed an interpretable machine learning algorithm, Eclass, to discriminate melanomas from nevi using dermoscopy images, aiming to provide transparency compared to black-box deep learning models. The researchers utilized two cohorts of dermoscopy images acquired in New York and Barcelona, training both Eclass and a convolutional neural network (CNN) on 668 images containing 113 melanomas and 236 nevi. Eclass operated on 38 engineered imaging biomarkers, whereas the CNN used raw pixel data, with model performance evaluated via the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) in a Monte Carlo simulation. The results showed that Eclass achieved an AUROC of 0.71 ± 0.07, outperforming the CNN's AUROC of 0.67 in 74.88% of trial runs. The study concludes that Eclass offers a more interpretable and efficient alternative for melanoma detection, potentially facilitating faster clinical adoption by allowing physicians to understand the diagnostic cues.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on discriminating melanomas from nevi. The dataset included 113 melanomas. Melanomas were defined by invasive histopathological diagnoses.

The study discriminated melanomas from nevi. The dataset included 236 nevi. These were pigmented lesions that did not demonstrate a benign pattern under dermoscopy but were histopathologically confirmed as nevi (including atypical nevi).

all_task_types.details: The paper describes a binary classification task to discriminate melanomas from nevi in dermoscopy images. Both the interpretable machine learning algorithm (Eclass) and the deep learning classifier (CNN) were trained to predict a melanoma probability (between 0 and 1) for histopathological diagnoses. The primary evaluation metric was the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC), which is standard for classification tasks. The text explicitly refers to 'skin cancer classification' in keywords and 'classification of each lesion' in the methods.

datasets_used.name: New York Cohort
datasets_used.description: A cohort of dermoscopy images of primary melanoma skin cancers versus abnormal nevi acquired in New York using an EpiFlash dermatoscope attached to a Nikon D80 camera. Images were alcohol-coupled and nonpolarized.

use.training: Combined with the Barcelona cohort to form the training set for Eclass and CNN models.
use.validation: Combined with the Barcelona cohort to form the validation set (Validation Set 1) for performance evaluation.
use.testing: Combined with the Barcelona cohort to form the testing set (hold-out test set in Monte Carlo simulation).
use.other: Part of the initial dataset collection for the study.

datasets_used.name: Barcelona Cohort
datasets_used.description: A cohort of digital dermoscopy images of pigmented lesions acquired in Barcelona, Spain, using a Dermlite Foto dermatoscope. All lesions did not demonstrate a benign pattern under dermoscopy.

use.training: Combined with the New York cohort to form the training set for Eclass and CNN models.
use.validation: Combined with the New York cohort to form the validation set (Validation Set 1) for performance evaluation.
use.testing: Combined with the New York cohort to form the testing set (hold-out test set in Monte Carlo simulation).
use.other: Presented for the first time in this study.

datasets_used.name: Combined Study Dataset (Validation Set 1)
datasets_used.description: The combined dataset of 668 dermoscopy images from the New York and Barcelona cohorts, reduced to 349 images (one lesion per patient/image) by filtering out images with hair, surgical ink, or other features preventing biomarker computation. This dataset contained 113 melanomas and 236 nevi. It was used for the primary analysis reported in the paper.

use.training: Used to train both the Eclass and CNN models.
use.validation: Used as 'Validation Set 1' to characterize performance (AUROC) via Monte Carlo simulation with hold-out test sets for each iteration.
use.testing: Used as the hold-out test set within the Monte Carlo simulation iterations.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC)
Partial area under the ROC curve

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the diagnostic performance (AUROC) of the Eclass and CNN models by randomly drawing ROCs from the respective model sets to determine if one outperformed the other.
To estimate the uncertainty of the AUROC metrics for both the Eclass and CNN models.
To determine the statistical significance of imaging biomarkers in discriminating melanomas from nevi, using a significance threshold of p < 0.05.
To evaluate the statistical significance of imaging biomarkers across different datasets (cross-validation sets 1 and 2) and compare them to the original published data.
To analyze the relationship between clinician age and the utility score of the App (R2 = 0.41).
To characterize the performance (AUROC) of the models on the validation set.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical significance testing of imaging biomarkers (p-value)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: Imaging biomarkers do not have diagnostic significance in discriminating melanomas from nevi.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: Imaging biomarkers have diagnostic significance in discriminating melanomas from nevi.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Imaging biomarkers were considered statistically significant if p < 0.05. The study reported that a similar number of imaging biomarkers were statistically significant across cross-validation sets and the original published data set, indicating that the biomarkers behave similarly across datasets.

Combined Study Dataset (Validation Set 1.description: 349 dermoscopy images (113 melanomas, 236 nevi) from New York and Barcelona cohorts.
Combined Study Dataset (Validation Set 1.metric: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)

comparative_results.method: Monte Carlo simulation comparing randomly drawn AUROCs from 1000 Eclass ROCs vs 10 CNN ROCs

Combined Study Dataset (Validation Set 1.notes: Eclass operated on 38 imaging biomarkers; CNN operated on raw pixels. Eclass was more computationally efficient. The AUROC for both models was noted to be lower than in other studies with larger numbers of more typical nevi.

Clinician Utility Study.description: Human subjects research involving 10 clinicians aged 26 to 64 years old testing the Eclass App.
Clinician Utility Study.notes: The diagnostic score was the least favorite feature. Scores were favored by younger clinicians.

Previously Published Data (Reference).description: Comparison to previously published Eclass results.
Previously Published Data (Reference).notes: The current study's AUROC (0.71) was lower than the 0.91 published for melanoma detection based on different training data. The previous study used branching logic to achieve 98% sensitivity.

architecture_used.main_model: Two models: Eclass (interpretable machine learning ensemble classifier) and CNN (Convolutional Neural Network, specifically ResNet-50).
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 weights pre-trained on the ImageNet database for transfer learning.
architecture_used.input_size: CNN: Raw dermoscopy images (New York cohort: 1-5 megapixels; Barcelona cohort: 5.9 x 2.7 megapixels mean). Eclass: 38 engineered imaging biomarkers (IBCs).

Eclass.description: Implements a 'wisdom of the crowd' approach sampling predictions from a broad range of machine learning predictive models. No convolutional aspect.
Eclass.base_classifiers: 12 statistical/machine learning algorithms (including C5.0 decision tree, logistic regression, etc., as referenced in Table 2 of the paper).
Eclass.final_score: Median risk score produced by eight independent machine learning algorithms.

CNN.description: Modified with output layers designed for binary classification.

CNN.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but standard for ResNet-50 in this context.
CNN.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but implied binary classification loss.
CNN.class_imbalance_handling: Minority class (melanoma) oversampling to train on an equivalent number of melanoma and nevi images.
CNN.normalization: Pixel values normalized to zero mean and standard deviation of 1.
CNN.inference_augmentation: Test time augmentation: class predictions generated for five randomly augmented versions of each test image; majority vote used as final predicted class.
CNN.early_stopping: Trained until validation dataset accuracy had not improved for ten epochs.
CNN.training_time: 52 hours on an Nvidia Quadro M5000 GPU.

Eclass.input_features: 38 imaging biomarkers achieving diagnostic statistical significance (p < 0.05), including 4 multicolor (MC) and 34 single-color (SC) IBCs.

classification_or_output_head.CNN: Output layers designed for binary classification (melanoma probability between 0 and 1).
classification_or_output_head.Eclass: Outputs a melanoma probability (between 0 and 1) derived from the median risk score of the ensemble.

approximate_parameters.Eclass: Not explicitly reported, but described as having a model parameter size to data size ratio in the underparameterized 'classical' regime.

preprocessing_techniques: Filtering out images with hair, surgical ink, or other features preventing biomarker computation (resulting in 319 discarded images from the initial dataset)
Requirement for images to show the complete lesion with full borders and some adjacent normal skin
Exclusion of images containing bubbles in the immersion media optically coupling the imaging device to the skin
Pixel values normalized to zero mean and standard deviation of 1 (for CNN)
Data augmentation: Flip, zoom, and rotate (for CNN training)
Test time augmentation: Class predictions generated for five randomly augmented versions of each test image with majority vote used as final predicted class (for CNN inference)
Minority class (melanoma) oversampling to train on an equivalent number of melanoma and nevi images (for CNN)
Hand-segmentation of dermoscopic color segments within the published data set blind to gold standard diagnoses for color metric generation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Flip, zoom, and rotate were used during CNN training.
Minority class (melanoma) oversampling was used during CNN training to ensure the model was trained on an equivalent number of melanoma and nevi images.
During inference, class predictions were generated for five randomly augmented versions of each test image, and the majority vote was used as the final predicted class.

conclusions: The interpretable machine learning algorithm (Eclass) outperformed the deep learning classifier (CNN) in 74.88% of trial runs, achieving a higher mean AUROC (0.71 vs 0.67) on the validation dataset.

Eclass is computationally more efficient than the CNN, requiring 150 seconds for training compared to 52 hours for the CNN, although this excludes the ~3 hours required to calculate the imaging biomarkers.
The diagnostic performance of both models was lower than in previous studies with larger numbers of typical nevi, indicating limitations in generalization to this specific dataset.

Eclass offers a more interpretable alternative to black-box deep learning models, allowing physicians to understand the diagnostic cues (imaging biomarkers) and potentially embrace the technology faster.
Imaging biomarker cues can be effectively used during sensory cue integration in clinical screening, providing a manageable amount of machine learning-augmented information for dermatologists.
The Eclass App was rated with a mean utility score of 2.3 out of 4 by clinicians, with younger clinicians favoring the app more than older clinicians.
The diagnostic score feature was the least favorite aspect of the Eclass App among the tested clinicians.

The study suggests that automated, unconstrained visual-aided screening with optimized decision trees is feasible for human use without computer vision, potentially reducing computation time and complexity.

Eclass and imaging biomarkers are not yet sufficiently generalized for real-time analysis from mobile phone-coupled dermatoscopes, highlighting a limitation for immediate clinical deployment in that context.
important_remarks: The study's AUROC (0.71) was significantly lower than the 0.91 reported in previous publications using different training data, indicating limited generalizability to this specific dataset with atypical nevi.
The dataset was small (349 images: 113 melanomas, 236 nevi) and filtered to exclude images with hair, surgical ink, or bubbles, which may introduce selection bias and limit real-world applicability.
The CNN model was trained on a class-imbalanced dataset using minority class (melanoma) oversampling, which may artificially inflate performance metrics compared to natural prevalence.
The Eclass training time (150 seconds) excluded the ~3 hours required to calculate the imaging biomarkers, underestimating the total computational cost relative to the CNN's 52-hour training time.
The study lacks external validation on independent, unseen cohorts from different geographic regions or imaging devices beyond the combined New York/Barcelona validation set.

The CNN architecture (ResNet-50) was not optimized for this specific task (e.g., no hyperparameter tuning reported beyond standard transfer learning), potentially creating an unfair comparison against the specialized Eclass model.
The clinical utility study involved only 10 clinicians, limiting the statistical power and generalizability of the user acceptance findings.

The Eclass model's performance is described as 'more sensitive than specific' (lower false positives than false negatives), which may not align with clinical priorities where avoiding unnecessary biopsies (high specificity) is often critical.

The study acknowledges that imaging biomarkers are not yet sufficiently generalized for real-time analysis from mobile phone-coupled dermatoscopes, highlighting a barrier to immediate clinical deployment.

The 'diagnostic score' feature was the least favored aspect of the App by clinicians, suggesting that raw probability outputs may be less useful than the visual biomarker cues for clinical decision-making.
